title: Knowledge Sharing Infrastructure
description: Map the field's knowledge dissemination channels — from peer-reviewed journals to practitioner communities — and understand the speed-credibility tension every sophisticated practitioner must navigate.

sections.title: Academic Journals: The Field's Credibility Infrastructure
sections.content:
Peer-reviewed publication is where the cyberdelic field's evidence base is formally established. Understanding which journals publish relevant work, and what their different roles are, helps practitioners evaluate sources and stay current without getting lost in the literature.

VR-Specific Journals

  • Frontiers in Virtual Reality: Open-access, relatively fast review cycles, covers the full range of VR research including therapeutic and consciousness applications. A good first destination for platform-specific research.
  • Presence: Teleoperators and Virtual Environments (MIT Press): The field's oldest dedicated VR journal, with particular depth on presence, immersion, and embodiment research. High prestige; slower publication timelines.
  • Cyberpsychology, Behavior, and Social Networking: Covers the psychological and behavioral effects of digital technology, including VR. Strong on mechanism and phenomenology research.

General Science Journals (High Impact)

  • Nature and Science: The field's most prestigious publications. Cyberdelic-adjacent work appears rarely but carries enormous credibility when it does.
  • PLOS ONE: Open-access, rigorous peer review, publishes across scientific disciplines. Published the original Isness-D mystical experience study. High credibility, freely accessible to anyone.

Publication Lag: The typical timeline from study completion to published results is 1–2 years, sometimes longer. In a fast-moving field, this means the published literature is always somewhat behind current practice. Practitioners need to hold both the rigor of published evidence and the reality that the field's working knowledge is ahead of its published knowledge simultaneously.

sections.title: Conferences and Foundational Books
sections.content:
Beyond their community functions, conferences serve a specific knowledge dissemination role: they are where unpublished and recent findings are first presented publicly. Conference presentations often precede publication by 6–18 months, making them a leading indicator of where the field is heading. The trade-off is that conference presentations haven't completed peer review; they carry less evidentiary weight than published papers.

Key Conferences for Knowledge Dissemination

  • SIGGRAPH: Computer graphics and interactive technology; where VR rendering and display research appears earliest.
  • IEEE VR: The technical conference for VR researchers; highest concentration of presence and immersion research.
  • Psychedelic Science (MAPS): Where psychedelic and cyberdelic research intersects; consciousness mechanism research appears here.
  • Science and Nonduality (SAND): Where philosophical and phenomenological dimensions of consciousness technology are discussed.

Foundational Books

  • Experience on Demand by Jeremy Bailenson (2018): The most accessible and authoritative overview of VR psychology research, written by Stanford's VHIL director. Required reading for understanding how VR affects human psychology and why it works as a medium for transformation.
  • Infinite Reality by Blascovich and Bailenson (2011): Earlier but still relevant exploration of VR's social and psychological implications; complements Experience on Demand with more theoretical depth.
  • Altered Traits by Goleman and Davidson (2017): Not specifically about VR, but essential context — the definitive review of meditation science's evidence base. Understanding what meditation actually does neurologically provides the framework for evaluating what cyberdelic VR might amplify or replicate.

sections.title: Online Knowledge Sharing and Standards Development
sections.content:
Research Access Tools

  • PubMed: The primary database for biomedical and life sciences research. Free public access to abstracts; starting point for any evidence-based platform evaluation.
  • Google Scholar: Broader than PubMed, including conference papers and preprints. Useful for finding the full citation network around a specific study.
  • ResearchGate: Researcher networking platform where authors often share full-text versions of papers that would otherwise be paywalled. Also useful for seeing what researchers are working on before publication.

Practitioner Rapid Sharing

Reddit and Discord serve a different knowledge function than academic databases — they circulate working knowledge fast. A platform issue discovered by ten facilitators this week will appear in r/cyberdelics before it appears in any journal. The trade-off is signal/noise: peer consensus in community forums is faster but less reliable than peer review. The sophisticated practitioner uses both.

Standards Development (Slow but Consequential)

  • ISO is developing VR safety standards — a slow process (3–5 years per standard) but one that will eventually provide authoritative technical baselines for hardware performance, exposure limits, and safety protocols.
  • Professional associations in the cyberdelic space are establishing competency criteria and ethical guidelines — the practitioner-facing standards that will eventually parallel clinical licensing requirements.

Critical Knowledge Gaps the Field Most Needs to Fill

  • Long-term outcome studies (what happens 6 months after a cyberdelic protocol?)
  • Diverse population research (almost all current studies used primarily white, educated, neurotypical samples)
  • Mechanism clarification (we know cyberdelic VR produces effects; we're still building understanding of exactly why)
  • Best practice consensus across facilitation models
  • Integration method effectiveness research

sections.title: Reading the Knowledge Landscape
sections.content:
The cyberdelic field's knowledge infrastructure has a structural characteristic that practitioners need to internalize: speed and credibility trade off against each other, almost without exception.

A peer-reviewed journal article in PLOS ONE carries high credibility — the methodology has been independently assessed, the statistics reviewed, the claims tested. But the result you're reading was probably produced 2–4 years before you're reading it.

A Reddit post from a facilitator with seven years of practice carries real knowledge: experiential, current, specific. But it hasn't been verified, it may reflect one person's unusual case, and experienced facilitator isn't a peer review process.

Between these poles: conference presentations (faster than journals, not yet reviewed), professional association guidelines (authoritative but slower than practitioner communities), foundational textbooks (deep and vetted but potentially dated on specific findings).

Neither end of the spectrum is sufficient alone. The practitioner who only reads journals is always behind current practice reality. The practitioner who only reads community forums is navigating without an evidence foundation. The sophisticated practitioner moves fluidly between sources, using the right tool for the question at hand.

The Knowledge Construction exercise below will ask you to practice exactly this — sorting sources by their position on the speed-credibility spectrum.

modules.title: The Knowledge Landscape: Speed vs. Credibility

content.title: The Knowledge Landscape: Speed vs. Credibility
content.instruction: Every knowledge source in the cyberdelic field sits somewhere on a spectrum between high credibility / slow dissemination and fast dissemination / lower credibility. Drag each source into the category that best reflects its position on this spectrum. Some placements are straightforward anchors — others require nuanced reasoning about peer review, selection processes, and access models.

items.text: Peer-reviewed journal article (e.g., study in Frontiers in Virtual Reality)
Foundational textbook (e.g., Experience on Demand by Jeremy Bailenson)
PLOS ONE open-access study (peer-reviewed, publicly free)
Conference presentation at IEEE VR (pre-publication research)
Professional association guidelines (emerging cyberdelic facilitator ethics standards)
Reddit post from an experienced cyberdelic facilitator describing a novel platform application
Discord community discussion among active cyberdelic practitioners

content.completionText:
The Pattern Is the Point

Notice what you built: the sources with the highest credibility are almost all slow — validated, but delayed. The fastest sources are almost all variable in quality — current, but unverified. This isn't a flaw in the field's knowledge infrastructure; it's a structural feature of how knowledge works in any rapidly evolving domain.

The practitioner who only works from the high-credibility / slow category will always be 2–3 years behind the current state of the field. The practitioner who only works from the fast / lower-credibility category is navigating without a verified map.

Your job as a sophisticated cyberdelic practitioner is to triangulate: use community sources to stay current and identify emerging patterns, use peer-reviewed sources to verify and ground those patterns in evidence, use foundational texts to maintain theoretical coherence. This two-category framework is something you can apply to every piece of information you encounter in this field for the rest of your career.
